does: This paper addresses hallucination in vision-language models by proposing a stage-wise preference optimization framework that constructs targeted multimodal training data near failure boundaries. Rather than using generic instruction-following datasets, the approach progressively generates hallucination-focused preference pairs emphasizing ambiguous spatial relationships, object interactions, OCR uncertainty, and adversarial false premises. Hallucinated negatives are created as minimally perturbed yet visually inconsistent alternatives, which are then used with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to better distinguish grounded reasoning from plausible but false outputs. The method is evaluated on open-source benchmarks and real-world multimodal scenarios, demonstrating improvements in grounding consistency and reduced hallucination rates.
connects: This work builds on the long-standing problem of hallucination in autoregressive language models, where likelihood maximization under joint probabilistic modeling produces outputs that are syntactically coherent but semantically grounded in neither image nor world knowledge. It extends recent advances in preference optimization methods, particularly Direct Preference Optimization, which has shown promise in aligning model outputs with human preferences without reinforcement learning. The paper responds to prior work identifying specific hallucination patterns in vision-language models—such as objects not present in images, incorrect spatial relationships, and false OCR—and combines insights from data construction strategies that focus learning on difficult boundary cases with preference-based fine-tuning approaches gaining adoption in multimodal settings.
enables: This framework enables future work on more targeted and efficient alignment of vision-language models by demonstrating how stage-wise data construction can focus optimization on high-failure regions. The approach opens avenues for systematically identifying and mitigating other failure modes in multimodal systems beyond hallucination, such as reasoning errors or cross-modal inconsistencies. The method could inspire development of automated hallucination detection and synthetic data generation pipelines that continuously adapt to model weaknesses. It also points toward better evaluation metrics and benchmarks that probe grounding consistency across multiple modalities, ultimately advancing the reliability and trustworthiness of vision-language models for safety-critical applications.
